questions.questionText: A 45-year-old woman presents with bone pain. X-ray shows bilateral, symmetrical radiolucent bands perpendicular to the cortex at the medial femoral neck. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Looser zones (pseudofractures/Milkman fractures) are PATHOGNOMONIC of osteomalacia. Why correct: Bilateral, symmetrical radiolucent bands perpendicular to cortex = Looser zones. Common sites: medial femoral neck, pubic rami, lateral scapular border, ribs. Why A wrong: Osteoporosis shows generalized radiolucency and compression fractures, NOT Looser zones. Why C wrong: Paget's shows coarsened trabeculae, cortical thickening, and bowing — not perpendicular lucent bands. Why D wrong: Hyperparathyroidism shows subperiosteal resorption, not pseudofractures. Clinical relevance: Osteomalacia = defective mineralization of mature bone (adult equivalent of rickets). Exam trap: Don't confuse Looser zones with stress fractures — Looser zones are bilateral and symmetrical. Memory aid: 'LOOSEr zones = LOOSE mineralization = OsteoMALACIA.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Metabolic Bone Disease.

questions.questionText: A 15-year-old boy presents with knee pain. X-ray shows a mixed lytic-sclerotic lesion in the distal femoral metaphysis with periosteal elevation forming a triangle and a 'sunburst' pattern. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumor. Why correct: Age 10-20, metaphysis around knee (distal femur #1), Codman triangle (periosteal elevation) + sunburst/sunray pattern are classic. Why A wrong: Ewing sarcoma shows 'onion-peel/laminated' periosteal reaction in diaphysis of long bones, age 5-15. Why C wrong: GCT occurs age 20-40 in EPIPHYSIS (touching articular surface), appears as 'soap bubble' lytic lesion. Why D wrong: Chondrosarcoma occurs in older adults (>40), pelvis/proximal femur, with ring-and-arc calcifications. Clinical relevance: Elevated ALP due to osteoblastic activity. Exam trap: Codman triangle is NOT specific to osteosarcoma (seen in any aggressive periosteal process) but sunburst + Codman + metaphysis + age = osteosarcoma. Memory aid: 'OsteoSARComa = SUN-burst + CodmAn + Around Knee.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: A 10-year-old boy presents with fever and diaphyseal bone pain. X-ray shows a permeative lytic lesion in the femoral diaphysis with 'onion-peel' periosteal reaction. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Ewing sarcoma — diaphyseal tumor in children with laminated/onion-peel periosteal reaction. Why correct: Age 5-15, diaphysis of long bones (femur most common), permeative destruction + onion-peel periosteal reaction is classic. Why A wrong: Osteosarcoma is metaphyseal with sunburst pattern. Why B wrong: Osteomyelitis is in metaphysis; while it can produce periosteal reaction, the clinical history of a soft tissue mass + permeative pattern favors Ewing. Why D wrong: Osteochondroma is a benign exostosis (most common benign bone tumor), no destruction. Clinical relevance: t(11;22) translocation. Exam trap: Ewing mimics osteomyelitis (fever, elevated WBC) — if imaging shows diaphyseal permeative lesion, think Ewing first. Memory aid: 'Ewing = onion-pEELing in diaphysis, 11;22.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: A 30-year-old woman presents with knee pain. X-ray shows an expansile lytic lesion in the proximal tibial epiphysis extending to the subarticular surface with a 'soap-bubble' appearance. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: GCT — epiphyseal lytic lesion in skeletally mature patients. Why correct: Age 20-40, epiphysis touching articular surface (subarticular), expansile lytic 'soap-bubble' appearance, around knee (distal femur/proximal tibia). Why A wrong: Osteosarcoma is metaphyseal, age 10-20, with sunburst. Why B wrong: ABC is eccentric, expansile, in metaphysis, younger age. Why D wrong: Chondroblastoma is epiphyseal BUT in skeletally IMMATURE (open physis, <20 years) with stippled calcification. Clinical relevance: Locally aggressive, 50% around knee. Campanacci grading. Exam trap: Both GCT and chondroblastoma are epiphyseal — differentiate by AGE (GCT: closed physis >20, chondroblastoma: open physis <20). Memory aid: 'GCT = Giant at epiphysis, Closed physis, Thirty-year-old.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: X-ray of the skull in a 55-year-old man shows multiple well-defined 'punched-out' lytic lesions without sclerotic margins. Serum protein electrophoresis shows an M-spike. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Multiple myeloma — 'punched-out' lytic lesions WITHOUT reactive sclerosis. Why correct: Age >40, multiple well-defined lytic lesions without surrounding sclerosis, M-spike on SPEP confirms monoclonal gammopathy. Skull, spine, pelvis most common sites. Why A wrong: Metastases usually have irregular margins and may show mixed lytic-sclerotic pattern. Why C wrong: HPT shows subperiosteal resorption (radial side of middle phalanges), salt-and-pepper skull. Why D wrong: Eosinophilic granuloma (Langerhans cell histiocytosis) is in younger patients (<30). Clinical relevance: Bone scan may be NEGATIVE in myeloma (no osteoblastic activity) — use skeletal survey or PET-CT. Exam trap: Myeloma is the exception to 'bone scan for skeletal metastases' — skeletal survey is preferred. Memory aid: 'Myeloma = Multiple Punched-out, M-spike, No Sclerosis, bone scan Misses it.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: Which bone tumor characteristically shows 'ring-and-arc' pattern of calcification on X-ray?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Chondroid matrix calcification has ring-and-arc (C-shaped/comma-shaped) pattern. Why correct: Chondrosarcoma — malignant cartilage tumor in >40 years, pelvis/proximal femur. Ring-and-arc calcification = chondroid matrix mineralization. Why A wrong: Osteosarcoma shows osteoid matrix (cloud-like/amorphous dense calcification), sunburst. Why C wrong: Ewing sarcoma — permeative destruction, no matrix calcification typically. Why D wrong: Fibrosarcoma shows permeative lysis without matrix calcification. Clinical relevance: Any cartilage tumor (enchondroma, osteochondroma, chondrosarcoma) shows ring-and-arc. Exam trap: Enchondroma (benign) and chondrosarcoma (malignant) both show ring-and-arc — differentiate by size, endosteal scalloping, and cortical destruction. Memory aid: 'Chondroid = C-shaped arCs and Rings.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: A mother brings an infant with a swelling on the skull since birth. X-ray shows a well-defined lytic lesion in the occipital bone with beveled edges and a 'button sequestrum.' Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: LCH shows punched-out skull lesions with beveled edges and button sequestrum. Why correct: LCH (eosinophilic granuloma) in children — well-defined lytic skull lesion with beveled/scalloped edges (inner table destroyed more than outer) and central bone fragment (button sequestrum). Why A wrong: Dermoid near suture lines, no button sequestrum. Why C wrong: Epidermoid shows sclerotic margins. Why D wrong: Hemangioma of skull shows 'sunburst/spoke-wheel' pattern. Clinical relevance: LCH spectrum includes eosinophilic granuloma (single bone), Hand-Schüller-Christian (triad: skull lesions, DI, exophthalmos), Letterer-Siwe (disseminated). Exam trap: Button sequestrum is a central bony fragment within a lytic lesion — don't confuse with sequestrum in osteomyelitis. Memory aid: 'LCH = Lytic Calvarial lesion with Holes (beveled).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: A 60-year-old man with prostate cancer has back pain. Bone scan shows multiple areas of increased uptake throughout the axial skeleton. X-ray shows dense sclerotic lesions in the vertebral bodies. What type of metastases does prostate cancer produce?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Prostate cancer produces OSTEOBLASTIC (sclerotic) metastases. Why correct: Prostate and breast (treated) produce predominantly sclerotic/osteoblastic metastases — dense white lesions on XR, intensely hot on bone scan. Why A wrong: Purely lytic metastases — kidney (most lytic), thyroid, lung, melanoma. Why C wrong: Mixed — breast (most common) and lung can produce mixed pattern. Why D wrong: Expansile — renal and thyroid metastases. Clinical relevance: Bone scan detects osteoblastic mets; PET-CT better for lytic mets. Exam trap: Most common cancers to metastasize to bone = BLT-KP (Breast, Lung, Thyroid, Kidney, Prostate). Prostate is sclerotic; kidney/thyroid are lytic. Memory aid: 'Prostate Puts Sclerosis in Bone; Kidney Kills bone (lytic).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Bone Metastases.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is the most sensitive imaging modality for detecting early avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head?
questions.explanation: Core concept: MRI is the most sensitive and specific imaging for AVN. Why correct: MRI detects marrow edema and necrosis BEFORE x-ray changes appear (sensitivity >95%, specificity >98%). Classic sign: 'double-line sign' on T2 (inner bright line = granulation tissue, outer dark line = sclerosis). Why A wrong: X-ray only shows AVN in later stages (Ficat II-IV: sclerosis, crescent sign, collapse). Why B wrong: CT — better for subchondral fracture (crescent sign) but less sensitive than MRI for early changes. Why D wrong: Bone scan — 'cold in hot' pattern but lower sensitivity/specificity than MRI. Clinical relevance: Ficat/ARCO classification. Causes: steroids (#1), alcohol, SLE, sickle cell, trauma. Exam trap: X-ray may be NORMAL in early AVN — always do MRI if clinically suspected. Memory aid: 'MRI = Most Ready Indicator of AVN.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – AVN.
questions.conceptTag: AVN MRI double-line sign femoral head

questions.questionText: A 65-year-old man presents with progressive bowing of the tibia and hearing loss. X-ray shows coarsened trabeculae, cortical thickening, and expansion of the tibia. Serum ALP is markedly elevated. Most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Paget's disease — disorganized bone remodeling causing coarsened, thickened, deformed bone. Why correct: Elderly male, bowing deformity, hearing loss (skull involvement → CN VIII compression), coarsened trabeculae, cortical thickening, elevated ALP (markedly — reflecting osteoblastic activity). Why A wrong: Osteomalacia shows Looser zones and osteopenia, not cortical thickening. Why C wrong: Fibrous dysplasia — 'ground-glass' matrix, shepherd's crook deformity of proximal femur, younger age. Why D wrong: Osteopetrosis — uniformly dense/sclerotic bones ('marble bone'), not coarsened trabeculae. Clinical relevance: Stages: lytic→mixed→sclerotic. Complications: pathological fractures, osteosarcoma (1% — most feared), hearing loss. Exam trap: Both Paget's and fibrous dysplasia cause bowing — Paget's has COARSENED trabeculae; fibrous dysplasia has GROUND-GLASS appearance. Memory aid: 'Paget = Pagoda of Thick coarse bone + very high ALP.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Paget's Disease.

questions.questionText: A 12-year-old boy has a bony hard, non-tender swelling near the knee growing away from the joint. X-ray shows a sessile bony projection from the proximal tibial metaphysis with a cartilage cap. The cortex of the lesion is continuous with the parent bone cortex. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Osteochondroma is the most common benign bone tumor. Why correct: Sessile/pedunculated bony exostosis growing away from the joint, cortical and medullary continuity with parent bone (pathognomonic), cartilage cap. Around knee, adolescent. Why A wrong: Osteosarcoma is destructive and intra-medullary with aggressive periosteal reaction. Why C wrong: GCT is epiphyseal, lytic, age 20-40. Why D wrong: Osteoid osteoma is small (<1.5cm), intensely painful (especially at night, relieved by aspirin), with central nidus. Clinical relevance: Malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma if cartilage cap >2cm in adults. Multiple hereditary exostoses (MHE) has higher transformation risk. Exam trap: Cortical continuity is the KEY feature — if cortex is NOT continuous, it's not osteochondroma. Memory aid: 'OsteoChondroma = Cortical Continuity Cap.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Benign Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: A 20-year-old presents with severe nocturnal thigh pain completely relieved by aspirin. CT scan of the femur shows a small (<1.5 cm) lucent nidus surrounded by dense reactive sclerosis in the cortex. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Osteoid osteoma — small (<1.5cm), painful lesion relieved by NSAIDs/aspirin. Why correct: Classic triad — nocturnal pain, aspirin relief, central nidus (<1.5cm) with surrounding sclerosis. Cortical location in diaphysis of long bones (femur/tibia most common). Why A wrong: Osteoblastoma is similar but LARGER (>2cm), occurs in posterior elements of spine, NOT relieved by aspirin. Why C wrong: Brodie abscess is a chronic osteomyelitis cavity, usually metaphyseal, with history of infection. Why D wrong: Stress fracture — linear lucency without a nidus, activity-related pain. Clinical relevance: CT is imaging of choice (best for nidus identification). Prostaglandin E2 production by nidus → pain. Treatment: radiofrequency ablation. Exam trap: Osteoid osteoma vs Osteoblastoma — SIZE differentiates (1.5cm cutoff). Both have nidus. Memory aid: 'Osteoid OsteOma = Only One cm, Ouched at night, Out with aspirin.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Benign Bone Tumors.

questions.questionText: X-ray of the hand in a patient with hyperparathyroidism most characteristically shows:
questions.optionB: Subperiosteal resorption along the radial aspect of the middle phalanges
questions.explanation: Core concept: Subperiosteal bone resorption is the EARLIEST and MOST SPECIFIC radiological sign of hyperparathyroidism. Why correct: Radial aspect of middle phalanges of 2nd and 3rd fingers — subperiosteal resorption (lace-like erosion of cortex). PTH excess → osteoclastic resorption. Why A wrong: Periosteal new bone is seen in conditions like hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy (HPOA). Why C wrong: Looser zones = osteomalacia. Why D wrong: Sunburst = aggressive tumors (osteosarcoma). Clinical relevance: Other HPT signs — salt-and-pepper skull, Brown tumors (osteoclastomas), dental lamina dura loss, chondrocalcinosis. Exam trap: The RADIAL side of MIDDLE phalanges — most specific site. Not proximal phalanges. Memory aid: 'PTH Pours away Periosteal bone on Radial middle phalanges.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Metabolic Bone Disease.

questions.questionText: A child presents with a 'shepherd's crook' deformity of the proximal femur. X-ray shows a well-defined intramedullary lesion with homogeneous 'ground-glass' matrix. Diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Core concept: Fibrous dysplasia — 'ground-glass' matrix with skeletal deformity. Why correct: Ground-glass/hazy matrix (fibrous tissue replaces normal bone), shepherd's crook deformity of proximal femur (coxa vara), well-defined intramedullary. Monostotic (70-80%) or polyostotic. Why A wrong: Paget's has COARSENED trabeculae and cortical thickening, older adults. Why C wrong: Simple/unicameral bone cyst — purely lytic (NO matrix), 'fallen fragment sign.' Why D wrong: Enchondroma — ring-and-arc calcification (chondroid matrix), hands. Clinical relevance: McCune-Albright syndrome: polyostotic FD + café-au-lait spots (coast-of-Maine) + precocious puberty. Exam trap: Ground-glass vs coarsened trabeculae = fibrous dysplasia vs Paget's. Memory aid: 'Fibrous Dysplasia = FD = Frosted glass + Deformity (shepherd's crook).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Fibrous Dysplasia.

questions.questionText: A neonate with generalized increased bone density on X-ray and pathological fractures is diagnosed with osteopetrosis. On the lateral spine X-ray, there is a characteristic appearance of the vertebral bodies described as:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Rugger-jersey spine — dense bands at endplates with central lucency (sandwich appearance). Why correct: Osteopetrosis (marble bone disease) — failure of osteoclast function → diffusely dense sclerotic bones. Rugger-jersey spine: alternating dense (endplates) and relatively lucent (central) bands. Why A wrong: Codfish vertebra — biconcave vertebral bodies in osteoporosis/osteomalacia. Why C wrong: Ivory vertebra — uniformly dense single vertebra (Paget's, lymphoma, metastasis). Why D wrong: Bamboo spine — fused vertebrae in ankylosing spondylitis (syndesmophytes + squaring). Clinical relevance: Autosomal recessive (malignant, infantile) vs autosomal dominant (benign, adult). Complications: pancytopenia (marrow replaced by bone), CN palsies (foraminal narrowing). Exam trap: Rugger-jersey spine also seen in renal osteodystrophy — differentiate by clinical context. Memory aid: 'Osteopetrosis = marble bones = Rugby-jersey stripes on spine.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Metabolic Bone Disease.

questions.questionText: A 35-year-old with ankylosing spondylitis shows which classic X-ray finding in the spine?
questions.optionB: Bamboo spine (bilateral symmetrical syndesmophytes and facet joint fusion)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Bamboo spine is the classic late finding in ankylosing spondylitis. Why correct: AS — bilateral symmetrical marginal syndesmophytes (thin, vertical ossifications bridging vertebral bodies) + facet joint fusion + ligamentous ossification → 'bamboo spine.' Starts at SI joints (bilateral sacroiliitis — earliest finding). Why A wrong: Osteophytes = degenerative spondylosis (horizontal projections, asymmetric). Why C wrong: Schmorl's nodes = disc herniation through vertebral endplate (degenerative). Why D wrong: Spondylolisthesis = forward slippage of vertebra (pars interarticularis defect in young). Clinical relevance: HLA-B27 associated. Modified New York criteria. Exam trap: Syndesmophytes (AS — thin, vertical, marginal) vs osteophytes (spondylosis — thick, horizontal) vs parasyndesmophytes (psoriatic — thick, non-marginal). Memory aid: 'AS = Ankylosed Spine = Bamboo.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Spine.

questions.questionText: A 50-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis. Hand X-ray shows periarticular erosions at the MCP joints, periarticular osteopenia, and joint space narrowing with ulnar deviation. What is the EARLIEST radiographic finding?
questions.optionB: Periarticular soft tissue swelling and juxta-articular osteopenia
questions.explanation: Core concept: Earliest X-ray finding in RA is soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia. Why correct: Progression: soft tissue swelling + periarticular osteopenia → marginal erosions (bare area) → uniform joint space narrowing → subluxation/deformity → ankylosis (end stage). Why A wrong: Erosions are early but NOT the earliest — they follow soft tissue changes. Why C wrong: Subluxation and deformities are late findings. Why D wrong: Ankylosis is end-stage. Clinical relevance: RA predominantly affects MCP, PIP, wrist (spares DIP — differentiates from OA). Hallmark deformities: swan-neck, boutonnière, ulnar drift, Z-thumb. Exam trap: RA = UNIFORM joint space narrowing (inflammatory pannus) vs OA = NON-UNIFORM (focal cartilage loss). Memory aid: 'RA Radiograph progression: Swelling → Osteopenia → Erosions → Narrowing → Subluxation → Ankylosis (SOENSA).' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Arthritis.

questions.questionText: Pencil-in-cup deformity on X-ray of the hands is pathognomonic of:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Pencil-in-cup = psoriatic arthritis mutilans (severe bone resorption with telescoping). Why correct: Psoriatic arthritis — DIP joint involvement (differentiates from RA), asymmetric oligoarthritis, peripheral erosive arthritis. Pencil-in-cup: severe resorption of phalangeal tip (pencil) sitting in expanded base of adjacent bone (cup). Why A wrong: RA — MCP/PIP, bilateral symmetrical, NO DIP involvement. Why C wrong: Gout — rat-bite/overhanging margin erosions (Martel's sign), tophi, preserved joint space initially. Why D wrong: OA — subchondral sclerosis, osteophytes, asymmetric narrowing, DIP (Heberden's) but no erosions. Clinical relevance: Psoriatic arthritis has 5 patterns: DIP predominant, arthritis mutilans, symmetric polyarthritis, asymmetric oligoarthritis, spondylitis. Exam trap: Both psoriatic arthritis and OA affect DIP — psoriatic has erosive changes. Memory aid: 'Psoriatic = Pencil-in-cup + DIP + asymmetric.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Arthritis.

questions.questionText: A 45-year-old man with chronic knee pain. X-ray shows chondrocalcinosis (calcification in the menisci and hyaline cartilage). This finding is most associated with:
questions.explanation: Core concept: Chondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification) = CPPD/pseudogout. Why correct: CPPD — calcium pyrophosphate crystals deposit in cartilage (hyaline and fibrocartilage). X-ray: linear calcification in articular cartilage (chondrocalcinosis), most common in knee menisci, wrist triangular fibrocartilage (TFCC), symphysis pubis. Why A wrong: Gout — monosodium urate deposits. X-ray: erosions with overhanging margins (Martel sign), soft tissue tophi. Why C wrong: RA — periarticular erosions, no cartilage calcification. Why D wrong: OA can coexist but chondrocalcinosis is NOT a feature of primary OA. Clinical relevance: CPPD associations — hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, Wilson's, hypothyroidism, hypomagnesemia. Crystals: rhomboid, positively birefringent. Exam trap: MCC of acute monoarthritis: <40 years = gout, >65 years = CPPD. Memory aid: 'CPPD = Chondro-Calcification of PyroPhosphate Disease.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Arthritis.

questions.questionText: A 'hair-on-end' or 'crew-cut' appearance of the skull on X-ray is seen in:
questions.optionB: Thalassemia major (and other chronic hemolytic anemias — marrow expansion causing perpendicular periosteal spicules)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Hair-on-end/crew-cut skull = marrow expansion due to chronic hemolytic anemia. Why correct: Beta-thalassemia major — chronic hemolysis → compensatory marrow hyperplasia → widened diploic space → perpendicular periosteal spicules ('hair-on-end'). Also in sickle cell disease and other chronic anemias. Why A wrong: Osteopetrosis — uniformly dense skull ('marble bone'), not hair-on-end. Why C wrong: Paget's — thickened calvarium, 'cotton-wool' patches, osteoporosis circumscripta (lytic phase). Why D wrong: Myeloma — punched-out lytic lesions without sclerosis. Clinical relevance: Chipmunk facies (maxillary expansion) + hepatosplenomegaly + hair-on-end skull = thalassemia major. Exam trap: Hair-on-end is DIFFERENT from sunburst — hair-on-end = marrow expansion (benign); sunburst = aggressive periosteal reaction (tumor). Memory aid: 'Hair-on-end = Hemolytic Anemia = Thalassemia major.' Reference: Textbook of Radiology – Hematological Disorders.
